_meta.purpose: Long-form rubric for matching a job description to a 0-5 level per competency via embedding + cosine similarity. Embed each level 'description' and compare to the JD (or the JD's competency-relevant text). Highest cosine similarity wins; its level number is the score.
_meta.version: long (expanded descriptions for sharper embedding separation)
_meta.scale: 0 = competency irrelevant / explicitly minimal in the role; 5 = competency is central, high-stakes, expert-level
_meta.embedding_note: Embed the 'description' fields only. The 'definition' field is for humans and should NOT be embedded, to avoid polluting the level vectors with shared competency vocabulary.
_meta.design_note: Each level is expanded with concrete role types, activities, and the kinds of phrasing real job descriptions use at that intensity. The added text is distinguishing detail (different audiences, stakes, tasks), not repeated intensity adjectives, so adjacent levels stay separable. Level 0-1 describe the KIND of role rather than asserting 'not required', so real JDs that simply omit the competency still match against them.
_meta.known_limitation_negation: Pure cosine similarity keys off topic, not negation. A JD saying a skill is explicitly NOT required can still match a mid/high level. Use the optional LLM check to catch negation and relative emphasis.
_meta.known_limitation_calibration: Per-competency max similarity is not comparable across competencies (one may top out at 0.30, another at 0.80). Normalize per-competency before combining into any weighted average.

effective_communicator.definition: Demand the role places on verbal, written, interpersonal, and cross-cultural communication.

levels.0: This is a heads-down, independent role where work is performed largely in isolation and tasks are self-contained, technical, or manual. The postholder follows defined instructions and completes assignments on their own, without needing to write for an audience, present, or coordinate with others. Typical work includes solitary processing, monitoring, data entry, hands-on or back-of-house tasks, or executing specifications to the letter. There are no meetings to lead, no documents to produce for readers, and no contact with clients or the public. Success is measured purely by completed output, and a person could do the job well while speaking to almost no one.
levels.1: Communication is incidental and low-effort. The postholder exchanges brief, simple information with an immediate supervisor or a small, fixed team, mostly to confirm tasks, log progress, or ask straightforward questions. Written work is limited to short emails, checklists, shift notes, or status updates, and verbal interaction rarely goes beyond familiar colleagues. The role involves following written or spoken instructions rather than explaining, persuading, or presenting, and there is no contact with clients, partners, or external audiences. Clear basic interaction is helpful but the job does not stand or fall on communication skill.
levels.2: Routine communication within a team is a normal part of the day. The postholder coordinates regularly with colleagues, writes clear everyday emails and short reports, documents their work, and takes part in team meetings, stand-ups, or handovers. Audiences are internal and familiar, and the content is operational rather than persuasive. The role rewards being organized, responsive, and easy to work with, but communication supports the work rather than being its main feature. There is little need to tailor a message for unfamiliar readers, present formally, or manage difficult conversations.
levels.3: Communication is a regular and important part of the role, woven through much of the work. The postholder writes documents for varied readers, such as summaries, specifications, or guidance, presents updates to internal groups, and explains their work clearly to people who are not specialists in it. They coordinate across more than one team, facilitate discussions, and keep different parties aligned, so clarity, structure, and responsiveness genuinely affect outcomes. Gaps in communication would slow the work or cause confusion. The role suits someone comfortable translating between audiences and keeping information moving.
levels.4: Communication is central to the role and largely outward-facing. The postholder presents frequently, writes persuasively in proposals, pitches, or reports, and deals directly with clients, partners, or senior stakeholders. They are expected to read the room, adapt the same message for very different audiences, field questions in real time, and represent their team or function to people outside it. Strong influence, negotiation in lower-stakes settings, and relationship management are part of the job, and how things are communicated materially shapes results. This is the kind of role described as client-facing, stakeholder-facing, or communications-heavy.
levels.5: Communication is the core of the role and carries high stakes. The postholder regularly addresses large audiences, leads high-value client relationships, negotiates important agreements, manages conflict, and influences senior decision-makers, executives, or the public. Exceptional verbal and written fluency is essential, frequently across cultures, in sensitive situations, and under significant time or reputational pressure. They may act as a spokesperson, lead difficult negotiations, or drive alignment among powerful and competing parties. The success of the work depends directly on the quality and credibility of their communication, and the role would be impossible for a weak communicator.

global_citizen.definition: Demand the role places on cultural competency, ethical engagement, inclusion, and working across diverse people and communities.

levels.0: The work is uniform, internal, and self-contained, with no engagement across different cultures, communities, or backgrounds. The role does not involve serving varied populations, working with international counterparts, or contributing to inclusion in any deliberate way. Cultural awareness, ethical engagement with communities, and cross-cultural skill simply do not feature in the duties. A person could perform the job fully without ever adapting to a different cultural context or interacting beyond a narrow, homogeneous setting. Nothing in the role depends on understanding or bridging differences between people.
levels.1: Engagement with diversity is incidental rather than designed into the role. The postholder works mainly within a homogeneous local team and only occasionally encounters people from different backgrounds, with no particular expectation to adapt how they work. Basic everyday courtesy is enough, and no specific cultural knowledge, inclusive practice, or community engagement is asked for. The job neither serves diverse populations directly nor requires navigating different norms or values. Cultural skill might come up by chance but is never a stated requirement of the work.
levels.2: The role involves working respectfully within a diverse team or workplace. Basic cultural sensitivity, inclusive everyday conduct, and the ability to collaborate comfortably with colleagues from a range of backgrounds are expected. However, the substance of the work is not built around different communities, global contexts, or equity goals. The postholder is asked to be a considerate teammate rather than to demonstrate deep cultural competency or to design for diverse audiences. Inclusion shows up as good behavior within the team rather than as a core function of the job.
levels.3: Cultural competency is a meaningful and recurring part of the role. The postholder regularly works with people from varied backgrounds, adapts to different norms and communication styles, and is actively expected to contribute to an inclusive environment. A real portion of the work serves diverse users, clients, or communities, so being attuned to culture and difference affects how well the job is done. The role might involve supporting varied populations, collaborating across regions or groups, or factoring different needs into the work. Sensitivity to inclusion is part of doing the role well, not just a nicety.
levels.4: The role is substantially about engaging across cultures and communities. The postholder works with international colleagues, clients, or beneficiaries, navigates differing values, customs, and expectations, and is responsible for inclusive and ethical practice within their area. Much of the work depends on building trust across difference, tailoring approaches to varied groups, and representing the organization fairly to diverse stakeholders. They may lead inclusion efforts, manage relationships across cultures, or design programs and services for varied populations. Strong cultural competency and ethical, community-minded judgment are core to performing the role.
levels.5: Global and cultural fluency is essential and sits at the heart of the role. The work centers on serving diverse or international communities, building deep cross-cultural relationships, championing inclusion and equity, and engaging ethically with people across many backgrounds and contexts. The postholder is expected to lead across cultures, mediate competing values, and make decisions where cultural understanding and community impact are decisive. The role might involve global program leadership, community advocacy, diplomacy, or equity-driven mission work where sensitivity to culture is mission-critical. Without genuine cultural competency and an inclusive, ethical orientation, the role could not be carried out.

creative_innovator.definition: Demand the role places on imagination, generating novel ideas, creative problem-solving, and experimentation.

levels.0: The work follows fixed procedures and established rules, with no room for new ideas or experimentation. The postholder executes clearly defined tasks exactly as specified, and consistency, accuracy, and adherence to process are what the role values. There is no expectation to improve methods, propose ideas, or find new ways of doing things, and deviating from the set approach would usually be a problem rather than a contribution. Typical work is procedural, repeatable, and standardized. Imagination and originality play no part in success.
levels.1: Creativity is rarely called for. The work is largely routine and rule-bound, with occasional minor problem-solving inside well-defined limits, such as choosing between a few standard fixes. The postholder is not expected to generate ideas, rethink approaches, or experiment, and new approaches are neither sought nor rewarded. Most of the value comes from doing established tasks reliably. On the rare occasion something unusual comes up, there is usually a known procedure to fall back on rather than a need to invent a solution.
levels.2: Some practical problem-solving is part of the role. The postholder occasionally suggests small improvements to existing processes, troubleshoots within familiar territory, and adapts known methods to fit minor variations. Innovation is welcome but not a core expectation, and the bulk of the work still follows proven approaches. The role rewards being resourceful and willing to tweak how things are done, without requiring original ideas or genuine novelty. Improvements tend to be incremental and grounded in what already works.
levels.3: Creative problem-solving is a regular and expected demand. The postholder is asked to generate ideas, improve how things are done, and find workable solutions to non-routine problems within their area. They are expected to think beyond the obvious, propose better approaches, and adapt flexibly when standard methods fall short. The role values initiative and inventiveness applied to real problems, even if the work is not framed as a creative or design role. Coming up with fresh, practical ideas is part of doing the job well.
levels.4: Innovation is a major part of the role. The postholder designs new approaches, pitches ideas, experiments with unproven methods, and is expected to push beyond established practice to create something new. They take the lead on developing concepts, prototyping, or reimagining how work is done, and a meaningful share of their value comes from originality rather than execution alone. The role tolerates and even expects calculated risk and iteration, since not every new idea will work. This is the kind of role described as creative, design-led, product-building, or focused on developing new ideas and approaches.
levels.5: The role is fundamentally about invention and original thinking. The postholder is expected to originate novel products, ideas, or strategies, combine concepts across very different domains, take creative risks, and drive innovation as the central purpose of the job. They set creative direction, generate ideas that did not previously exist, and are judged largely on the novelty and impact of what they create. The work might involve founding, inventing, designing breakthrough offerings, or leading research and creative vision. Without strong imagination and a genuine drive to create the new, the role could not be performed.

critical_thinker.definition: Demand the role places on analysis, evaluation, reasoning, judgment, and connecting information to make decisions.

levels.0: Tasks are simple and prescribed, and the decisions that shape the work are made elsewhere. The postholder follows clear instructions and completes defined steps without needing to evaluate options, interpret information, or weigh trade-offs. The role values accurate execution rather than judgment, and there is a correct, known way to do each task. Problems, when they arise, are escalated rather than analyzed. Analytical reasoning is not part of the job.
levels.1: Minimal reasoning is needed. The work involves straightforward choices among a few obvious options, basic checking, and applying simple rules, but no real evaluation of evidence or comparison of trade-offs. The postholder mostly recognizes familiar situations and responds in the standard way. Where judgment is required, it is light and bounded, and harder calls are passed upward. The role rewards attention and consistency more than analysis.
levels.2: Some analytical thinking is expected as part of everyday work. The postholder interprets routine information, applies set criteria to make ordinary decisions, and identifies obvious problems within a familiar scope. They are trusted to make sensible day-to-day calls without checking everything, but the situations are mostly well understood and the right answer is usually discoverable with modest effort. Deeper evaluation, ambiguity, and high-consequence judgment are not central. The role values being level-headed and methodical within known boundaries.
levels.3: Critical thinking is a regular and important demand of the role. The postholder analyzes information, weighs options, diagnoses problems that are not obvious, and makes reasoned decisions that affect their own work and that of their team. They are expected to investigate causes, consider alternatives, and justify their choices rather than simply follow a script. The work involves genuine judgment under everyday uncertainty, where a poor decision has real but recoverable consequences. Sound reasoning is part of doing the job well.
levels.4: Rigorous analysis and judgment are central to the role. The postholder evaluates complex or ambiguous information, assesses risks and trade-offs, draws well-supported conclusions, and makes decisions with significant consequences. They are expected to handle situations with no clear right answer, reconcile competing considerations, and defend their reasoning to others. The work often involves analysis, strategy, diagnosis, or decision-making where the quality of thinking directly determines results. This is the kind of role described as analytical, strategic, or requiring strong problem-solving and judgment.
levels.5: Advanced reasoning is the core of the role and the basis on which it is judged. The postholder tackles highly complex, ambiguous, or high-stakes problems, synthesizes conflicting evidence, builds strategy, and makes critical judgments that others depend on. They operate where the questions are hard, the information is incomplete or contradictory, and the consequences of error are serious. The role might involve setting strategy, leading complex analysis, or making decisions that shape direction at a senior level. Deep analytical rigor and sound judgment under uncertainty are essential, and weak reasoning would make the role untenable.

reflective_future_focused.definition: Demand the role places on self-awareness, learning from feedback, growth mindset, goal and career orientation, and adaptability.

levels.0: Duties are fixed, short-term, and unchanging, with no expectation of learning, reflecting on performance, or planning ahead. The role asks the postholder to do a defined task the same way over time, and there is no development path, feedback loop, or forward-looking element built in. Self-awareness, goal-setting, and adaptability are not part of how the job works. A person could perform it indefinitely without growing, reflecting, or thinking about the future. The role values steady delivery of the same work rather than personal or professional development.
levels.1: Growth is incidental rather than expected. The postholder is mainly asked to do the job as taught, with little structured feedback and limited development beyond learning the basic tasks. Planning beyond the immediate work, reflecting on performance, or adapting to change is not really part of the role. Any improvement tends to happen naturally with repetition rather than through deliberate reflection or goal-setting. The work rewards reliably maintaining a known standard.
levels.2: Some openness to feedback and learning is expected. The postholder takes on occasional training, acts on basic feedback, and adjusts when asked, but self-direction, goal-setting, and long-term focus are limited. Development is mostly reactive, prompted by a manager or a process rather than driven by the individual. The role values being teachable and willing to improve, without demanding strong self-awareness or a forward-looking outlook. Reflection happens when prompted rather than as a habit.
levels.3: Continuous learning and reflection are a real and expected part of the role. The postholder is expected to seek feedback, develop their skills, set goals, and adapt as the work and its demands change. They are trusted to notice where they need to improve and to take some ownership of their own growth rather than waiting to be told. The role assumes the person will keep developing and will adjust to evolving expectations. A growth-minded, self-directed attitude genuinely helps in doing the job well.
levels.4: Strong self-awareness and a growth mindset are important to the role. The postholder is expected to actively reflect on their performance, pursue their own development, plan ahead, set and track meaningful goals, and adapt readily to evolving demands. They are trusted to manage their own progress, turn feedback into change, and stay oriented toward where the work and their skills need to go next. The role suits someone deliberate about learning and forward-looking in how they operate. Drift, complacency, or an inability to adapt would be a real liability.
levels.5: The role is built around growth, vision, and adaptability. The postholder must continuously reflect, learn rapidly, set and drive long-term direction, and consistently turn feedback into improvement, often in a fast-changing or self-directed environment. Strong self-awareness, foresight, and a deep development orientation are essential, both for their own performance and frequently for guiding others or the wider direction of the work. The role might involve charting strategy, navigating constant change, or operating with high autonomy where the ability to learn and adapt is decisive. Without genuine reflectiveness and a forward-looking, growth-driven mindset, the role could not be sustained.

career_ready.definition: Demand the role places on professional foundation, workplace readiness, established professional skills, polish, and social capital.

levels.0: The role is fully entry-level with complete training provided from the ground up. No prior experience, professional polish, qualifications, or networks are expected, and the postholder is not assumed to bring any workplace foundation with them. The job is designed for someone new to professional work, with structured onboarding and close guidance. Reliability and willingness to show up are enough to begin. Existing professional skills or contacts confer no real advantage because everything needed is taught on the job.
levels.1: Minimal professional readiness is needed. Basic reliability, punctuality, and a willingness to learn are the main requirements, and the postholder needs little prior experience and only limited professional polish. The role provides support and supervision, so well-developed workplace skills are helpful but not necessary. Some exposure to a work environment is a bonus rather than an expectation. The job suits people early in their working lives or new to the field.
levels.2: Some workplace foundation is expected. The postholder should show basic professionalism, dependable conduct, punctuality, and core workplace skills such as following through and working within a team. Modest prior exposure, through internships, part-time work, or similar, is helpful but not essential. The role assumes a person can carry themselves appropriately in a workplace without yet having a deep professional track record. Polish and networks are not required, but a baseline of work-readiness is.
levels.3: A solid professional foundation is required. The postholder is expected to bring established workplace skills, consistent professional conduct, and some relevant experience, and to operate with limited supervision. The role assumes a degree of self-management, sound professional habits, and the ability to handle responsibilities without close oversight. Some prior experience in a similar setting is genuinely expected rather than optional. The person should already be comfortable navigating a professional environment.
levels.4: Strong professional readiness is central to the role. It demands proven professional skills, a confident and credible workplace presence, relevant experience, and the ability to build effective working relationships and represent themselves well. The postholder is expected to operate with autonomy, manage their responsibilities professionally, and inspire confidence in colleagues and stakeholders. Relevant networks and the ability to navigate professional settings smoothly are real assets. The role suits an experienced professional rather than someone still building their footing.
levels.5: A highly developed professional profile is essential. The role requires extensive relevant experience, polished professionalism, strong industry networks, and the confidence, presence, and social capital to operate credibly at a senior level from day one. The postholder is expected to command respect, build and leverage relationships, and represent the organization with authority in high-stakes professional settings. Deep professional readiness, an established reputation, and the ability to navigate senior environments are core to the role. Someone without a substantial professional foundation could not credibly hold it.
